original_abstract:
This study presents a detailed characterization of non-methane organic compound (NMOC) emissions from a diverse range of globally significant biomass fuels, including peat, crop residues, and woods used in various cookstove designs. Utilizing high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) during the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), the researchers were able to identify and quantify a broad suite of gas-phase species, many of which are reported here for the first time in biomass burning smoke. By combining exact mass measurements with composition-sensitive calibration techniques and supplementary data from Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, the study positively or tentatively identifies 80–96% of the total NMOC mass for most major fuel types—a significant improvement over previous unit-mass resolution studies.

The findings highlight that oxygenated NMOCs are the dominant compound class across all fuel types. Detailed emission factors (EFs) and emission ratios are provided for several reactive families, including aromatic hydrocarbons, phenolics, and furans, many of which serve as critical precursors for secondary organic aerosol (SOA) and ozone formation. The analysis also uncovers various nitrogen-containing organic compounds, such as amides and amines, which may play a role in new particle formation. Furthermore, a comparison of cooking technologies demonstrates that traditional three-stone fires produce substantially higher emissions than more advanced "rocket" or gasifier stoves. These results offer an updated and more comprehensive dataset for biomass burning emissions, providing essential inputs for chemical transport models to better assess the atmospheric, climatic, and health impacts of smoke on local and global scales.
humanized_abstract:
Non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emissions from burning of globally significant biomass fuels were characterized in detail. A range of globally important biomass energy sources, including peat, crop residues and a wide variety of tropical biomass woods and their associated cookstove configurations, were investigated. A large number of gas-phase NMOCs species were quantified using high resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometry (PTR-TOF-MS) during the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4) campaign. By utilizing exact mass along with composition-sensitive calibrations and supplemental Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) information, species accounting for 80–96% of the total NMOC mass are confidently or tentatively assigned for the majority of the major fuels investigated.

Comparing Emissions from Different Biomass Sources Recently, researchers completed a study that identified and quantified compounds present in non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emissions from various biomass fuels. According to the study, oxygenated NMOCs are the largest class of compounds for all tested biomass fuels, and detailed emission factors (EFs) and emission ratios for several reactive NMOC families such as; aromatic hydrocarbons (AHs), phenolics (PHEs), and furans (furan) and their derivatives have been calculated for the first time. These species are all important SOA and O3 precursors and the latter two have not been previously quantified in BB emissions. Numerous nitrogen containing organic compounds including amides and amines were also identified. The relative importance of various cooking emissions was investigated. Generally, it was found that traditional three-stone fires result in greater overall emissions than the more modern ‘rocket’ or gasifier stoves. The recently released study not only updates the current knowledge base on BB emissions, but it also provides a more detailed inventory of emissions required for modeling their atmospheric, climatic and health impacts at both local and global scales.
